intro:
The firstborn (Hebrew bekôr; Greek prōtotokos) is the first son to open the womb, and in the biblical world he holds a place of unique honor: preeminence among his brothers, the leadership of the household, and a double share of the inheritance (Deuteronomy 21:17). "Firstborn" is therefore both a fact of birth and a title of rank — and Scripture repeatedly shows that the rank, not merely the birth order, is what God cares about, until at last the title comes to rest on Christ.

The firstborn belongs to the LORD in a special way. Every firstborn of Israel — of man and beast — is consecrated to God, a claim grounded in the night of the Passover, when the LORD struck the firstborn of Egypt but spared Israel's, and so the firstborn must be redeemed (Exodus 13:2; Exodus 13:13–15). Israel as a whole is named God's firstborn son: "Israel is my firstborn son… Let my son go that he may serve me" (Exodus 4:22–23). Yet the privilege can be forfeited and freely reassigned: Esau sells his birthright and Jacob receives it (Genesis 25:33), Reuben loses his by sin (Genesis 49:3–4), and again and again God elevates the younger over the elder — a sign that firstborn status is finally his gift of grace, not a human entitlement.

The title rises higher still in the royal promise. God declares of the Davidic king, "I will make him the firstborn, the highest of the kings of the earth" (Psalm 89:27). Here "firstborn" no longer means first in time but supreme in dignity — the appointed heir and ruler over all. The shadow is straining toward a person who will hold the rank in full.

That person is Christ. Paul calls him "the firstborn of all creation," not as a creature but as the heir and Lord over everything made, the one in whom and for whom all things exist (Colossians 1:15–16). He is "the firstborn from the dead," the first to rise to deathless life and so the head of a new humanity (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5). He is "the firstborn among many brothers," the pattern and elder brother of all whom God conforms to his image (Romans 8:29). When God brings this firstborn into the world, he commands, "Let all God's angels worship him" (Hebrews 1:6).

In Christ the shadow is fulfilled and then shared. He is the true firstborn son who, unlike Israel and unlike Adam's sons, never forfeits his birthright; through union with him, the redeemed become "the assembly of the firstborn who are enrolled in heaven" (Hebrews 12:23), heirs of the double portion of the Spirit and the inheritance. The motif matters because it tells us who Jesus is — the preeminent Heir and Lord of all — and what we become in him: adopted sons who share the status, dignity, and inheritance of God's beloved firstborn.
